addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

 - Messages are logged; no responses are sent back to publishers
 - If NATS client is unavailable, API subscriptions are skipped (logged as warning)
 
+### Security Considerations
+
+**The NATS API has no application-layer authentication or authorization.** Unlike
+the REST endpoints (which require a JWT with `RoleAPI`), the subscribers process
+any message delivered on the configured subjects. Anyone with publish rights to
+those subjects on the broker can:
+
+- Insert arbitrary jobs (potentially attributed to other users)
+- Mark running jobs as stopped, triggering archive/finalization
+- Overwrite node state and health metadata for any cluster
+
+Operators MUST restrict publish ACLs at the NATS broker (per-account or
+per-subject permissions) so that only trusted producers — e.g. the scheduler
+integration on a known host or service account — can publish to the configured
+`subject-job-event` and `subject-node-state` subjects. A shared, unrestricted
+NATS broker is not a safe deployment topology for this API. A startup warning
+is logged when these subscriptions are enabled.
+
 ## Development Guidelines
 
 ### Performance

Keygen uses crypto/rand correctly. I have enough to complete the audit. Let me do a final check on the user-supplied add-user password (no minimum length) and confirm there are no remaining concerns with the rate limiter (per IP+username — username enumeration / lockout bypass).

The audit is complete. Here are my findings.

## Security Audit: Cryptography, Secrets, Config, Dependencies

Overall the codebase is in good shape. No SEVERE/critical vulnerabilities found in scope. Cryptographic primitives are sound: Ed25519 JWTs, bcrypt password hashing, crypto/rand everywhere security-sensitive, modern TLS config, no weak algorithms (md5/sha1/des/ecb) anywhere, no `math/rand` for tokens/secrets. Dependencies are current. Below are the lower-severity observations worth noting.

### Findings

**1. LOW — Default/example secrets committed in tracked config files**
- `configs/config.json:29` — NATS `"username":"root","password":"root"`; `:57-67` metric-store tokens `"MySecret"`; `:45-46` S3 keys `"xx"`.
- `configs/config-demo.json:19` — a real signed admin+api JWT (`sub:demo, roles:[admin,api]`).
- These are tracked in git (`git ls-files` confirms), whereas the runtime `config.json` is correctly `.gitignore`d.
- Verified non-exploitable: the placeholders are obvious dummies, and I decoded the demo JWT — it is signed with the demo key shipped in `configs/env-template.txt` and already expired (exp 2026-04-09). So this is hygiene, not a live leak. Fix: keep these as `.example`/`.template` files and ensure operators never deploy with the shipped `env-template.txt` keys.

**2. LOW — `checkDefaultSecurityKeys()` only warns, doesn't block** — `cmd/cc-backend/main.go:217-224`. Detects the default `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` and the demo `SESSION_KEY` is similarly only soft-handled. With the default Ed25519 keypair public in the repo, anyone can forge admin JWTs. It only logs a `Warn`. Mitigating factor: `SESSION_KEY` absence is correctly fatal (`auth.go:189-193`). Suggested: also fatal-error (or require an explicit `-dev` opt-in) when the known default JWT keypair is in use, mirroring the SESSION_KEY treatment.

**3. LOW — No password strength/length enforcement for local accounts** — `cmd/cc-backend/main.go:226-244` (`addUser`) and `internal/api/user.go:180`. A local user can be created with an empty or trivial password; bcrypt is applied (`repository/user.go:150`, `bcrypt.DefaultCost`=10, fine) but no minimum policy. Login is rate-limited at 5/15min per IP+username (`auth.go:76`), which mitigates online brute force. Suggested: enforce a minimum length on local-password creation.

### Verified-safe (no action needed)
- **JWT verification**: `jwt.go:86-92` pins `SigningMethodEdDSA` (no alg-confusion). `jwtCookieSession.go:123,142` uses `WithValidMethods([EdDSA])` and correctly routes keys by issuer (`TrustedIssuer`→cross-login key, `""`→internal key, else reject). `jwtSession.go` HS256/HS512 path uses a separate dedicated `CROSS_LOGIN_JWT_HS512_KEY`, not the Ed25519 key — no HMAC/asymmetric key confusion.
- **Password compare**: bcrypt's `CompareHashAndPassword` is constant-time (`local.go:41`). No raw `==` comparisons on secrets/tokens anywhere; token equality is delegated to the JWT library's signature verification.
- **Command injection**: `internal/api/log.go:92` uses `exec.CommandContext("journalctl", args...)` — no shell, admin-role-gated (`:34`), and free-text params (`since`, `search`) are regex-allowlisted (`^[a-zA-Z0-9 :\-\.]+$`), numeric params bounds-checked. Safe.
- **LDAP injection**: filters use `ldap.EscapeFilter`/`ldap.EscapeDN` (`ldap.go:91,134`). Safe.
- **Secrets logging**: no password/private-key/secret values are logged; messages only report presence/absence of env vars. OIDC debug logs roles/claims (not tokens) at Debug level.
- **OIDC**: crypto/rand for state+nonce, PKCE S256, nonce replay check, ID-token verification — all correct (`oidc.go`).
- **TLS**: TLS 1.2 min, GCM cipher suites only; no `InsecureSkipVerify` anywhere.
- **Dependencies** (`go.mod`): `golang-jwt/jwt/v5 v5.3.1`, `golang.org/x/crypto v0.52.0`, `coreos/go-oidc/v3 v3.18.0`, `go-ldap/ldap/v3 v3.4.13` — all current, no known severe CVEs.

Net: no SEVERE findings. The three LOW items are deployment-hardening recommendations, with #2 being the most worthwhile (block startup on the publicly-known default JWT keypair).

content.text: These are under `MountAPIRoutes` (the `/api` group), which requires JWT with `RoleAPI` (and admin) — not session cookie, not regular users. So `metaData.message` can only be set by API-key holders (RoleAPI/admin). The `{@html}` rendering is thus a stored-XSS sink reachable only by privileged API users. That's a defense-in-depth concern, not a critical user-to-admin escalation. I now have everything needed. Let me compile the report. ## Security Audit Report — Web Layer (cc-backend) ### Summary The XSS posture of the server-side templates is **solid**: `web/web.go` uses `html/template` (not `text/template`), and there are **no** `template.HTML`/`template.JS`/`template.URL` wrappers anywhere. All user-controlled values (usernames, filter presets, job IDs, login redirect, error messages) are interpolated via plain `{{ .X }}` in their correct contexts (JS string, HTML attribute, HTML text), so Go's contextual auto-escaping applies. The login `redirect` is also validated server-side to be a relative path (`internal/auth/auth.go:389`), closing the open-redirect. JWTs handed to the frontend are kept in component memory, not `localStorage`/`sessionStorage` (`web/frontend/src/config/.../UserOptions.svelte`, `ShowUsersRow.svelte`) — good. The real findings are around **missing CSRF defense-in-depth, missing security headers, and a stored-XSS sink**. --- ### Finding 1 — No CSRF tokens on cookie-authenticated state-changing endpoints (relying solely on SameSite=Lax) **Severity: Medium-High** **Files:** - `internal/api/rest.go:147-160` (`MountConfigAPIRoutes`: `createUser`, `deleteUser`, `updateUser`, `editNotice`, `runTagger` — all session/admin-auth) - `internal/api/rest.go:164-171` (`MountFrontendAPIRoutes`: `/frontend/configuration/` POST, `/frontend/jwt/` GET) - `cmd/cc-backend/server.go:228` (GraphQL `/query` mutations, session-auth) - `internal/auth/auth.go:323-324` (cookie `SameSite=Lax`, `HttpOnly`) **Analysis:** These endpoints authenticate via session cookie (`AuthViaSession`) with **no CSRF token** (confirmed: the only `csrf` reference in the codebase is the OIDC PKCE comment). The previous audit's `SameSite=Lax` does block classic cross-site **form POST** CSRF, so the POST/PUT/DELETE config endpoints are *mostly* covered by that alone. The concern: - `GET /frontend/jwt/?username=` mints a valid JWT and returns it in the body. Under `SameSite=Lax`, a cross-site **top-level GET navigation** *does* send the cookie. An attacker page can't read the response cross-origin (CORS `AllowCredentials:false`), so token theft is blocked — but this shows the reliance on a single control. Any future state-changing GET handler would be exploitable. - Defense-in-depth: `SameSite=Lax` is the *only* CSRF control. Browsers/proxies that don't enforce it, or a future endpoint added as GET, would be exposed. - `getJWT` (`rest.go:325`) lets non-admins mint a token only for themselves, and `ProvideJWT` (`jwt.go:122`) copies only the user's existing roles — no privilege escalation. `updateConfiguration` writes are scoped to the caller's own row via parameterized SQL (`userConfig.go:118`).

```go
    query = query.Offset((uint64(page.Page) - 1) * limit).Limit(limit)
}
```
- Exploit: a holder of any API token calls `GET /api/jobs/?items-per-page=-1&with-metadata=true`. ## Security Audit: SQL / Data Access Layer (`internal/repository/*.go`)

### FINDING 1 — SQL injection via metric name in JSON-path interpolation (SEVERE / Critical)

**Files / lines:**
- `internal/repository/stats.go:962` and `stats.go:969` (`jobsMetricStatisticsHistogram`)
- `internal/repository/jobQuery.go:343-347` (`buildFloatJSONCondition`, reached from `BuildWhereClause` for `filter.MetricStats`)

**Root cause:** A user-supplied metric name is concatenated directly into a SQLite `json_extract(footprint, "$.<name>")` path, which sits inside a double-quoted SQL string literal. A `"` in the input breaks out of the literal. No sanitization or whitelisting is applied on this path (unlike the `OrderBy` path, this code never calls `toSnakeCase`).

stats.go:959-969:
```go
binQuery := fmt.Sprintf(`CAST(... json_extract(footprint, "$.%s") ... )`, (metric + "_" + footprintStat), ...)
... .Where(fmt.Sprintf(`json_extract(footprint, "$.%s") is not null and ... <= %f`, (metric + "_" + footprintStat), ..., peak))
```

**Input trace (confirmed user-controlled):**
- GraphQL `jobsStatistics(filter, metrics: [String!], ...)` and `jobsMetricStats(filter, metrics: [String!])` → resolver `internal/graph/schema.resolvers.go:749` → `Repo.AddMetricHistograms(ctx, filter, metrics, ...)` → `stats.go:736 jobsMetricStatisticsHistogram(ctx, m, ...)`. `metrics` is a raw `[String!]` GraphQL arg with no enum/validation (`api/schema.graphqls:318`, `models_gen.go:168`).

Reachable by **any authenticated user** (RoleUser). `SecurityCheck` only adds a row-level `WHERE`; it does not constrain the injected fragment, and a `UNION`/comment can discard it.

**Exploit (verified with a SQLite PoC in-repo):** metric name
```
x") UNION SELECT secret FROM job--
```
produced SQL `... json_extract(footprint, "$.x") UNION SELECT secret FROM job--") ...` and exfiltrated data from another column. Any authenticated user can run arbitrary SQL: read other users' jobs, dump `hpc_user` rows (roles, projects, emails), etc.

**Fix:** Whitelist metric names against the configured cluster metric list (`archive.GetMetricConfig` / `archive.Clusters[].MetricConfig`) before use, rejecting unknown names. As defense-in-depth, validate the JSON path with a strict regex (`^[A-Za-z0-9_]+$`) in `jobsMetricStatisticsHistogram` and `buildFloatJSONCondition` and reject otherwise. Note `footprintStat` is config-derived (safer) but `metric` must be validated.

**File / lines:** `internal/repository/jobQuery.go:54,58,60,69,71` with `toSnakeCase` at `jobQuery.go:430-440`.

content.text:
`OrderByInput.field` is a free `String!`. It flows into `OrderBy(fmt.Sprintf("job.%s ASC", field))` after `toSnakeCase`. The char blacklist blocks string-literal breakout but permits parentheses/commas — enabling injection like `field = "id ASC, (SELECT CASE WHEN(...) THEN id ELSE ... END)"`. Wait — `toSnakeCase` lowercases everything, which breaks `SELECT`/`CASE` keywords? No, SQL keywords are case-insensitive in SQLite, so `select`/`case` still work. Spaces are blocked though, which makes building a subquery hard (SQL needs whitespace between tokens). Comments `/**/` could substitute for spaces, and `/` `*` are allowed. This is a real but harder-to-exploit vector. I'll report it as a secondary finding (medium-high) since the blacklist is fragile.

I have a complete picture. Here is my report.

---

## Security Audit: SQL / Data Access Layer (`internal/repository/*.go`)

### FINDING 1 — SQL injection via metric name in JSON-path interpolation (SEVERE / Critical)

**Files / lines:**
- `internal/repository/stats.go:962` and `stats.go:969` (`jobsMetricStatisticsHistogram`)
- `internal/repository/jobQuery.go:343-347` (`buildFloatJSONCondition`, reached from `BuildWhereClause` for `filter.MetricStats`)

**Root cause:** A user-supplied metric name is concatenated directly into a SQLite `json_extract(footprint, "$.<name>")` path, which sits inside a double-quoted SQL string literal. A `"` in the input breaks out of the literal. No sanitization or whitelisting is applied on this path (unlike the `OrderBy` path, this code never calls `toSnakeCase`).

stats.go:959-969:
```go
binQuery := fmt.Sprintf(`CAST(... json_extract(footprint, "$.%s") ... )`, (metric + "_" + footprintStat), ...)
... .Where(fmt.Sprintf(`json_extract(footprint, "$.%s") is not null and ... <= %f`, (metric + "_" + footprintStat), ..., peak))
```

**Input trace (confirmed user-controlled):**
- GraphQL `jobsStatistics(filter, metrics: [String!], ...)` and `jobsMetricStats(filter, metrics: [String!])` → resolver `internal/graph/schema.resolvers.go:749` → `Repo.AddMetricHistograms(ctx, filter, metrics, ...)` → `stats.go:736 jobsMetricStatisticsHistogram(ctx, m, ...)`. `metrics` is a raw `[String!]` GraphQL arg with no enum/validation (`api/schema.graphqls:318`, `models_gen.go:168`).
- Independently, GraphQL `JobFilter.metricStats: [MetricStatItem!]` → `MetricStatItem.metricName: String!` → `jobQuery.go:257 buildFloatJSONCondition(ms.MetricName, ...)`, same unsanitized `"$."+jsonField` concatenation.

Reachable by **any authenticated user** (RoleUser). `SecurityCheck` only adds a row-level `WHERE`; it does not constrain the injected fragment, and a `UNION`/comment can discard it.

**Exploit (verified with a SQLite PoC in-repo):** metric name
```
x") UNION SELECT secret FROM job--
```
produced SQL `... json_extract(footprint, "$.x") UNION SELECT secret FROM job--") ...` and exfiltrated data from another column. Any authenticated user can run arbitrary SQL: read other users' jobs, dump `hpc_user` rows (roles, projects, emails), etc.

**Fix:** Whitelist metric names against the configured cluster metric list (`archive.GetMetricConfig` / `archive.Clusters[].MetricConfig`) before use, rejecting unknown names. As defense-in-depth, validate the JSON path with a strict regex (`^[A-Za-z0-9_]+$`) in `jobsMetricStatisticsHistogram` and `buildFloatJSONCondition` and reject otherwise. Note `footprintStat` is config-derived (safer) but `metric` must be validated.

---

### FINDING 2 — Fragile char-blacklist on ORDER BY column (Medium-High)

**File / lines:** `internal/repository/jobQuery.go:54,58,60,69,71` with `toSnakeCase` at `jobQuery.go:430-440`.

`order.Field` (GraphQL `OrderByInput.field: String!`, free string, `api/schema.graphqls:468`) is passed through `toSnakeCase`, then interpolated into `OrderBy("job.%s ASC")` / `OrderBy("JSON_EXTRACT(footprint, \"$.%s\") ...")`. `toSnakeCase` uses a **deny-list** (blocks `' \ " ; -` and space) rather than an allow-list. It permits `(`, `)`, `,`, `/`, `*`, `=`, digits, letters. SQLite keywords are case-insensitive (the `ToLower` does not neutralize them), and `/**/` can substitute for blocked spaces. This leaves room for ORDER-BY-clause injection (e.g. boolean/error-based subqueries) and is one byte away from being trivially bypassable if a new caller is added or the blacklist drifts.

**Fix:** Replace the blacklist with a strict allow-list: validate `field` against the known sortable column set (the same whitelist pattern already used safely in `stats.go` via `sortBy2column`/`groupBy2column` maps). Reject anything not in the map.

---

### FINDING 3 — Identity values interpolated into SQL in tag counting (Medium)

**File / lines:** `internal/repository/tags.go:316-325` (`user.Username` into scope `IN (...)`) and `tags.go:333` (`strings.Join(user.Projects, "\",\"")` into a subquery `project IN (...)`).

`user.Username` and each `user.Projects` entry are wrapped in `"..."` and concatenated into the SQL string instead of being parameterized. These are identity attributes; for local accounts they are admin-set, but for OIDC/LDAP-provisioned users a username/project containing `"` would break out (or corrupt the query). Severity is lower because it is self-scoped and the values are typically provisioned by trusted IdP/admin, but it is still raw interpolation of externally-sourced strings into SQL.

**Fix:** Parameterize: build `sq.Eq{"t.tag_scope": scopeList}` for the scope `IN`, and `sq.Eq{"job.project": user.Projects}` (squirrel expands slices to placeholders) for the project subquery, matching the parameterized style already used at `tags.go:335` and in `jobQuery.go` `SecurityCheckWithUser`.

---

### Areas reviewed and found clean
- `node.go`, `user.go`, `jobCreate.go`, `job.go`, `config.go`, `userConfig.go`: all DB access uses squirrel with `?` placeholders or `r.DB.Exec/Query` with bound args. The `fmt.Sprintf` hits in `job.go`/`jobCreate.go` are LRU cache keys, not SQL.
- `jobQuery.go` `buildStringCondition`, `buildMetaJSONCondition`, `buildResourceJSONCondition`: JSON `jsonField` args are **hardcoded literals** (`"jobName"`, `"hostname"`) and all values are parameterized — safe.
- `jobQuery.go:204,215,234` (`job_state`, `shared`, `array_job_id` inlined): values come from validated GraphQL enums / typed int, not free strings — acceptable.
- `stats.go` `durationExpr`/threshold/bin/`sortBy2column`/`groupBy2column`: all interpolated values are server-side integers/timestamps or map-whitelisted column names — safe.
- `dbConnection.go`, `migration.go` `fmt.Sprintf`: config-derived connection/DSN params, not user input.
- `jobFind.go:299,310,335`: builds a frontend URL query string, not SQL.

**Priority:** Finding 1 is the urgent one — unauthenticated-of-data, fully attacker-controlled, trivially exploitable arbitrary SQL by any logged-in user, verified working. Fix that first.

`RootDir` is trusted config; cluster/host are attacker-controlled. The root cause is confirmed and self-contained. I have enough to report. Let me do a final quick check on the JSON checkpoint loader (read side) to note the read-path traversal at startup is from local dirs (trusted), and confirm the write path is the real issue.

I have completed the audit. Here are my findings.

## Security Audit: File Handling / Path Traversal — cc-backend

### SEVERE — Path traversal in metric-store checkpoint/WAL writes via unsanitized `cluster`/`host` tags

**Root cause:** The metric ingestion path takes `cluster` and `host` directly from attacker-supplied InfluxDB line-protocol tags with no validation, stores them verbatim as keys in the in-memory level tree, and later uses them to build filesystem paths with `path.Join(RootDir, cluster, host)`. `path.Join` *cleans* `..` but does **not** confine to the root — `path.Join("/var/checkpoints", "../../../../etc", "cron.d")` resolves to `/etc/cron.d` (verified empirically). The process then calls `os.MkdirAll` and `os.OpenFile(..., O_CREATE|O_WRONLY)` on that path.

**Attacker input source (both unauthenticated and low-priv):**
- `pkg/metricstore/lineprotocol.go:245-258` — `DecodeLine` reads `cluster=` / `hostname=`/`host=` tags into `cluster`, `host` with no checks (only the *measurement* name is whitelisted against `ms.Metrics`).
- Reachable via NATS subscription (`ReceiveNats`, lineprotocol.go:45) — **no application-layer auth at all** (documented in CLAUDE.md).
- Reachable via REST `POST /api/write` (`internal/api/metricstore.go:91`, route `internal/api/rest.go:133`) — requires a `RoleAPI` JWT, but the cluster name there comes from the line-protocol body tag, not the validated `?cluster=` default, so even an API user can target arbitrary paths.

**Sink locations:**
- `pkg/metricstore/walCheckpoint.go:247` — `hostDir := path.Join(Keys.Checkpoints.RootDir, msg.Cluster, msg.Node)` → `os.MkdirAll(hostDir, ...)` (line 210) + `os.OpenFile(path.Join(hostDir,"current.wal"), O_CREATE|O_APPEND|O_WRONLY)` (line 216).
- `pkg/metricstore/walCheckpoint.go:749` — `hostDir := path.Join(dir, path.Join(selectors[i]...))` where `selectors` are the raw tree keys (`sel1,sel2` from `m.root.children` at lines 682-686) → binary snapshot `os.OpenFile(.../<ts>.bin.tmp, O_CREATE|O_WRONLY|O_TRUNC)` (walCheckpoint.go:782-785). `O_TRUNC` means an existing file at the traversed path is overwritten.
- `pkg/metricstore/checkpoint.go:251` + `:347-352` — identical issue for the legacy `"json"` checkpoint format (`path.Join(dir, selectors...)` then `os.OpenFile(<ts>.json, O_CREATE|O_WRONLY|O_TRUNC)`).

**Concrete exploit:** Publish to the configured NATS job/metric subject (or POST to `/api/write` with an API token) a line such as:
```
cpu_load,cluster=../../../../var/spool/cron,hostname=crontabs value=1 1700000000
```
On the next WAL flush / checkpoint cycle the server creates `…/var/spool/cron/crontabs/current.wal` and `…/<ts>.bin` outside the checkpoint root, and `O_TRUNC` lets an attacker clobber arbitrary files writable by the service account. With a `host` like `x/../../etc` similar escapes apply. This is arbitrary file write (and directory creation) outside the intended checkpoint root, under the cc-backend service UID — potential for overwriting config/binaries/cron and thus code execution depending on deployment.

**Why this is a real gap (not theoretical):** The repository already added `validatePathComponent()` (`internal/api/rest.go:209-216`, blocks `..`, `/`, `\`) in the recent "Fix issues after security audit" commit and applied it to the `machine_state` handlers (rest.go:433-440, 485-491) which build `filepath.Join(MachineStateDir, cluster, host)`. The exact same class of input (cluster/host → path) on the metric-store ingestion path was **not** given the same treatment. The mitigation exists; it just wasn't applied here.

**Suggested fix:** Validate `cluster`/`host` at ingestion in `DecodeLine` before they enter the tree and WAL message — reuse the same rule as `validatePathComponent` (reject empty, `..`, `/`, `\`, and ideally restrict to an allowlist charset like `[A-Za-z0-9._-]`). Drop/skip the sample (and log) on violation, mirroring the existing measurement-name whitelist `continue`. Optionally also defense-in-depth at the sink: after `path.Join`, verify the result is still within `RootDir` (e.g. `strings.HasPrefix(filepath.Clean(hostDir)+sep, filepath.Clean(RootDir)+sep)`). Validating at ingestion is preferred since the same unsanitized strings also become tree keys used by the JSON and binary checkpoint writers.

---

### Areas reviewed and found clean

- **`pkg/archive/fsBackend.go` (job archive writes/reads):** `getDirectory`/`getPath` (lines 55-75) build paths from `job.Cluster` + integer `JobID`/`StartTime`. The job-start/stop REST and NATS flows route through `importer.SanityChecks` (`internal/importer/initDB.go:260-263`), which rejects any `job.Cluster` not matching an exactly-named configured cluster via `archive.GetCluster` (clusterConfig.go:176-183, exact `==` compare). `JobID`/`StartTime` are numeric. No traversal reachable from API input.
- **`LoadClusterCfg(name)` (fsBackend.go:478):** `name` comes only from `GetClusters()` (directory listing / trusted config), not API input.
- **S3 backend (`pkg/archive/s3Backend.go`):** keys mirror `job.Cluster` (validated) and integer IDs; cluster discovery is from bucket listing (trusted). No user-controlled traversal.
- **Parquet subpackage (`reader.go`, `writer.go`, `target.go`, `convert.go`):** `ReadFile`/`ListParquetFiles` operate on the trusted local archive directory during import/conversion, not on per-request API input. No zip/tar extraction or decompression-bomb surface (Parquet column readers are bounded by library; no untrusted archive unpacking).
- **Checkpoint *read* paths** (`checkpoint.go:381-413`, walCheckpoint.go loaders): traverse the local checkpoint directory via `os.ReadDir` (trusted filesystem), not request input.
- **`machine_state` REST handlers** (`internal/api/rest.go:424-498`): correctly guarded by `validatePathComponent` for both `cluster` and `host`.
- **Deserialization:** JSON unmarshals into fixed typed structs (no `interface{}`/gob/dynamic type dispatch); job-data/meta optionally validated against JSON Schema. No unbounded-recursion or type-confusion path observed. (Note: line-protocol ingestion has no body-size limit, but that's a DoS concern out of scope for this traversal-focused review.)
- **Symlinks:** no `os.Readlink`/symlink-following logic; `os.Create`/`O_CREATE` follow existing symlinks at the target, which compounds the traversal finding above but is not an independent issue.

**Bottom line:** One severe finding — unvalidated `cluster`/`host` line-protocol tags flowing into checkpoint/WAL filesystem paths (`pkg/metricstore/lineprotocol.go` → `walCheckpoint.go:247,749` / `checkpoint.go:251,347`), giving arbitrary file write/overwrite outside the checkpoint root via NATS (unauthenticated) or `/api/write` (RoleAPI). The fix already exists in the codebase (`validatePathComponent`) and simply needs to be applied at metric ingestion. The job-archive, S3, and parquet paths are properly gated by exact cluster-name validation.

I have enough to report. Let me summarize the findings.

## Security Audit Results: GraphQL & NATS APIs

I read the in-scope code (`internal/graph/schema.resolvers.go`, `util.go`, `stats_cache.go`, `internal/api/nats.go`, `internal/api/node.go`, the GraphQL server setup in `cmd/cc-backend/server.go`, and the repository authorization layer in `internal/repository/jobQuery.go` / `user.go`).

### Authorization / IDOR (GraphQL) — Clean
The resolver layer is consistently authorized:
- All job-data resolvers (`Job`, `JobMetrics`, `JobStats`, `ScopedJobStats`, `Jobs`, `JobsStatistics`, `JobsMetricStats`, `JobsFootprints`, `rooflineHeatmap`) funnel through `repository.SecurityCheck` / `SecurityCheckWithUser` (`internal/repository/jobQuery.go:146`), which restricts non-admin/support users to their own jobs (or project jobs for managers). `Job` additionally re-checks ownership at `schema.resolvers.go:491`. I traced the grouped-stats and histogram paths (`stats.go:245,353,441,502,566,764,830,971`) — all apply `SecurityCheck`.
- `User` query and `UserData` job resolver go through `FetchUserInCtx` (`internal/repository/user.go:515`), which returns `"forbidden"` when a non-privileged user requests another user's record.
- `NodeMetrics`, `NodeMetricsList`, `ClusterMetrics` explicitly require Admin/Support (`schema.resolvers.go:825,892,955`). Node state/topology resolvers (`Node`, `Nodes`, `NodeStates`) are unrestricted, but they return cluster-wide operational metadata, not user-owned metric data — consistent with the product design, not an IDOR.

No IDOR or missing-authorization findings.

### Finding 1 — MEDIUM/HIGH: No GraphQL query complexity or depth limit (DoS)
`cmd/cc-backend/server.go:89-92` constructs the gqlgen handler with only `transport.POST` and no `extension.FixedComplexityLimit` (nor any depth guard). Any authenticated low-privilege user can submit deeply nested / high-fan-out queries. Concretely:
- `jobs(page: {itemsPerPage: -1})` bypasses the SQL `LIMIT` entirely (`internal/repository/jobQuery.go:78` — `-1` means "load all"), returning every job the user may see, and per returned job the client can also select expensive sub-resolvers (`metaData`, `footprint`, `concurrentJobs` → `FindConcurrentJobs`, and especially `metricData`/stats that hit the metric store). There is no upper bound on `itemsPerPage` for positive values either.
- Combined with alias duplication (e.g. requesting `jobsStatistics` many times under different aliases) a single request can multiply backend work.

Exploit: a single valid user issues one POST that forces unbounded DB + metric-store load, exhausting CPU/memory/connection resources (server-side DoS).

Suggested fix: register `graphQLServer.Use(extension.FixedComplexityLimit(N))` with per-field complexity weights in `gqlgen.yml` (high weights on `metricData`, `jobMetrics`, `concurrentJobs`, list resolvers), and enforce a maximum `ItemsPerPage` (reject or cap `-1` / very large values) in the `Jobs`/`NodeMetricsList` resolvers. A query-depth limit should also be added.

### NATS (`internal/api/nats.go`) — Clean (aside from the already-documented lack of auth)
I checked specifically for the non-auth issues you flagged:
- Malformed line protocol: `handleJobEvent`/`handleNodeState` (lines 199-225, 492-518) check `len(data)==0`, and `receivers.DecodeInfluxMessage` (cc-lib v2.12.0) returns errors rather than panicking; all errors are handled and the loop returns/continues. `GetTag`/`GetEventValue` return `(value, ok)` and are checked — no unchecked type assertions, no nil derefs, no slice indexing on attacker data.
- JSON payloads use `DisallowUnknownFields` and decode into typed structs; `handleStartJob` runs `importer.SanityChecks` (validates cluster against the known set, state validity, resources/user non-empty, non-negative counts) identical to the REST path.

Note: `ItemsPerPage == -1` means "load all" (no limit), and there is no enforced upper bound. Also `uint64(page.Page)` with a negative Page would underflow, but that's authenticated and just produces a bad offset.

I have enough to report. Let me summarize the findings.

## Security Audit Results: GraphQL & NATS APIs

I read the in-scope code (`internal/graph/schema.resolvers.go`, `util.go`, `stats_cache.go`, `internal/api/nats.go`, `internal/api/node.go`, the GraphQL server setup in `cmd/cc-backend/server.go`, and the repository authorization layer in `internal/repository/jobQuery.go` / `user.go`).

### Authorization / IDOR (GraphQL) — Clean
The resolver layer is consistently authorized:
- All job-data resolvers (`Job`, `JobMetrics`, `JobStats`, `ScopedJobStats`, `Jobs`, `JobsStatistics`, `JobsMetricStats`, `JobsFootprints`, `rooflineHeatmap`) funnel through `repository.SecurityCheck` / `SecurityCheckWithUser` (`internal/repository/jobQuery.go:146`), which restricts non-admin/support users to their own jobs (or project jobs for managers). `Job` additionally re-checks ownership at `schema.resolvers.go:491`. I traced the grouped-stats and histogram paths (`stats.go:245,353,441,502,566,764,830,971`) — all apply `SecurityCheck`.
- `User` query and `UserData` job resolver go through `FetchUserInCtx` (`internal/repository/user.go:515`), which returns `"forbidden"` when a non-privileged user requests another user's record.
- `NodeMetrics`, `NodeMetricsList`, `ClusterMetrics` explicitly require Admin/Support (`schema.resolvers.go:825,892,955`). Node state/topology resolvers (`Node`, `Nodes`, `NodeStates`) are unrestricted, but they return cluster-wide operational metadata, not user-owned metric data — consistent with the product design, not an IDOR.

No IDOR or missing-authorization findings.

### Finding 1 — MEDIUM/HIGH: No GraphQL query complexity or depth limit (DoS)
`cmd/cc-backend/server.go:89-92` constructs the gqlgen handler with only `transport.POST` and no `extension.FixedComplexityLimit` (nor any depth guard). Any authenticated low-privilege user can submit deeply nested / high-fan-out queries. Concretely:
- `jobs(page: {itemsPerPage: -1})` bypasses the SQL `LIMIT` entirely (`internal/repository/jobQuery.go:78` — `-1` means "load all"), returning every job the user may see, and per returned job the client can also select expensive sub-resolvers (`metaData`, `footprint`, `concurrentJobs` → `FindConcurrentJobs`, and especially `metricData`/stats that hit the metric store). There is no upper bound on `itemsPerPage` for positive values either.
- Combined with alias duplication (e.g. requesting `jobsStatistics` many times under different aliases) a single request can multiply backend work.

Exploit: a single valid user issues one POST that forces unbounded DB + metric-store load, exhausting CPU/memory/connection resources (server-side DoS).

Suggested fix: register `graphQLServer.Use(extension.FixedComplexityLimit(N))` with per-field complexity weights in `gqlgen.yml` (high weights on `metricData`, `jobMetrics`, `concurrentJobs`, list resolvers), and enforce a maximum `ItemsPerPage` (reject or cap `-1` / very large values) in the `Jobs`/`NodeMetricsList` resolvers. A query-depth limit should also be added.

### NATS (`internal/api/nats.go`) — Clean (aside from the already-documented lack of auth)
I checked specifically for the non-auth issues you flagged:
- Malformed line protocol: `handleJobEvent`/`handleNodeState` (lines 199-225, 492-518) check `len(data)==0`, and `receivers.DecodeInfluxMessage` (cc-lib v2.12.0) returns errors rather than panicking; all errors are handled and the loop returns/continues. `GetTag`/`GetEventValue` return `(value, ok)` and are checked — no unchecked type assertions, no nil derefs, no slice indexing on attacker data.

- Resource exhaustion via worker pools: `jobCh`/`nodeCh` are bounded buffered channels (`nats.go:100-101`) feeding a fixed worker count; backpressure blocks the NATS callback rather than spawning unbounded goroutines. Fine.
- Integer overflow: `job.Duration = int32(req.StopTime - job.StartTime)` (`nats.go:354`) could theoretically overflow for an absurd `StopTime`, but it is guarded by `job.StartTime > req.StopTime` (line 340) and matches the REST behavior; not separately exploitable beyond the already-documented unauthenticated-publish risk.

The NATS handlers faithfully mirror the REST handlers' validation; no new severe input-validation/panic/injection issues beyond the previously-documented authentication gap.

One real finding worth acting on: the **missing GraphQL complexity/depth limit and unbounded `itemsPerPage`** (DoS reachable by any authenticated user), in `cmd/cc-backend/server.go:89` plus the page handling in `internal/repository/jobQuery.go:78`. Authorization/IDOR coverage in the resolvers is solid, and the NATS handlers are clean against the input-validation/panic/injection class.

Confirmed. `Init()` returns `nil` (success) even when `CROSS_LOGIN_JWT_HS512_KEY` is unset, so the authenticator is always registered whenever `Keys.JwtConfig != nil`. My analysis is complete.

## Security Audit Findings — Authentication & Authorization

### CRITICAL — JWT session login accepts HMAC tokens forged with an empty key (auth bypass / admin takeover)

**File:** `internal/auth/jwtSession.go:27-39` (Init) and `:61-66` (Login), enabled via `internal/auth/auth.go:233-238`, reachable at `/jwt-login` and `/login` (`cmd/cc-backend/server.go:185-186`).

**The bug:**
- `JWTSessionAuthenticator.Init()` only sets `loginTokenKey` if `CROSS_LOGIN_JWT_HS512_KEY` is set, but it **always returns `nil` (success)**. So whenever `Keys.JwtConfig != nil`, this authenticator is registered in the chain even when no HS512 key is configured, leaving `loginTokenKey == nil`.
- In `Login()`, the keyfunc returns `ja.loginTokenKey` for `HS256`/`HS512` tokens. With golang-jwt v5, a `nil` `[]byte` passes the `key.([]byte)` type assertion and HMAC is computed over an empty key. I verified this with a standalone test against `golang-jwt/jwt/v5 v5.3.1`: a token signed `HS512` with a nil key parses as **valid**:
  ```
  FORGED TOKEN ACCEPTED: sub=attacker roles=[admin]
  ```
- `CanLogin` (`:41-49`) returns `true` for *any* request carrying an `Authorization` header or `login-token` query param, so the attacker's forged token reaches `Login()`.

**Exploit:** With the default config (`validate-user`/`ValidateUser` defaults to `false`) and `CROSS_LOGIN_JWT_HS512_KEY` unset, an unauthenticated attacker forges an HS512 JWT signed with an empty key containing `{"sub":"anyone","roles":["admin"]}`, sends it to `POST /jwt-login` as `Authorization: Bearer <token>` (or `?login-token=`), and gets a fully privileged admin session cookie. `getUserFromJWT(..., validateUser=false, ...)` trusts the attacker-controlled `sub` and roles verbatim. This is a complete unauthenticated privilege escalation. Even with `ValidateUser=true`, the attacker can still impersonate any existing username (session fixation as that user), though roles would come from the DB.

**Fix:** In `Init()`, return an error when `CROSS_LOGIN_JWT_HS512_KEY` is unset/empty so the authenticator is not registered (mirroring how `JWTCookieSessionAuthenticator.Init` returns errors when prerequisites are missing). Additionally, harden `Login()` to reject a nil/empty key:
```go
func (ja *JWTSessionAuthenticator) Init() error {
    pubKey := os.Getenv("CROSS_LOGIN_JWT_HS512_KEY")
    if pubKey == "" {
        return errors.New("CROSS_LOGIN_JWT_HS512_KEY not set: JWT session login disabled")
    }
    ...
}
```
and in the keyfunc: `if len(ja.loginTokenKey) == 0 { return nil, errors.New("HS login key not configured") }`.

---

### LOW — Roles from the standard JWT auth path are not validated against the known role set

**File:** `internal/auth/jwt.go:113-116`

When `ValidateUser == false`, `AuthViaJWT` sets `user.Roles = extractRolesFromClaims(claims, false)` — the `false` disables role-name validation, unlike `getUserFromJWT` which passes `true`. Arbitrary role strings from the token are accepted. The token must be signed with the server's own EdDSA key (`JWT_PRIVATE_KEY`), so this is not externally forgeable and is low severity, but it is an inconsistency worth aligning (`extractRolesFromClaims(claims, true)`).

---

### LOW — Per-user login rate limiter keyed on form `username`, ineffective for token-based logins

**File:** `internal/auth/auth.go:347-354`

The limiter key is `ip + ":" + username` where `username = r.FormValue("username")`. For `/jwt-login` (token/header logins) the username form field is empty, so all such attempts from one IP collapse to the `ip:` bucket — still per-IP limited (5 / 15 min), so brute-force is bounded, but there is no per-credential limiting for token logins. Minor; note in combination with the CRITICAL finding the rate limit is the only remaining brake on forged-token attempts.

---

### Areas reviewed and found clean

- **OIDC flow** (`oidc.go`): state cookie + form-state comparison (CSRF), PKCE (`S256ChallengeOption` + verifier cookie), nonce generation and ID-token nonce verification, ID token verified via `provider.Verifier` with `ClientID` audience check. Callback cookies are `HttpOnly`, `SameSite=Lax`, `Secure` when TLS. Roles are mapped through an allow-list switch (unknown roles dropped), so OIDC cannot inject arbitrary roles. Solid.
- **LDAP** (`ldap.go`): user search filter uses `ldap.EscapeFilter(username)` and bind DN uses `ldap.EscapeDN(...)` — injection-safe. Bind uses the user-supplied password correctly.
- **Local auth** (`local.go`): bcrypt compare; empty-hash LDAP users cannot reach it (CanLogin gated on `AuthViaLocalPassword`), and bcrypt rejects empty hashes anyway.
- **Main EdDSA JWT path** (`jwt.go:86-92`): pins `SigningMethodEdDSA`; a missing/short public key yields `ErrInvalidKey` (verified against the lib), so it fails safe — no algorithm-confusion or empty-key issue here.
- **Cookie session JWT** (`jwtCookieSession.go`): the previously-fixed `ParseUnverified` + issuer switch + `WithValidMethods` is correct and uses distinct keys per issuer.
- **Role gating** in `AuthAPI`/`AuthUserAPI`/`AuthMetricStoreAPI`/`AuthConfigAPI` (`auth.go:435-569`) requires `RoleAPI` and appropriate combinations; no obvious bypass.

The CRITICAL HS512 empty-key issue is the one severe item missed by the prior audit and should be fixed before release.

52		user *schema.User,
53		rw http.ResponseWriter,
54		r *http.Request,
55	) (*schema.User, error) {

59		}

62			if t.Method == jwt.SigningMethodHS256 || t.Method == jwt.SigningMethodHS512 {

content.text:
All three top findings are now verified — two with working PoCs, the third by code inspection. Here's the audit result.

## Repeat security audit — verdict

**Yes, the previous (Opus 4.7) audit missed severe issues — including two that I'd rate higher than anything it fixed.** There's a clear pattern: the prior audit fixed *one instance* of a bug class but missed a *sibling code path* with the same (or worse) flaw.

- It fixed JWT algorithm/issuer confusion in `jwtCookieSession.go` — but missed the **sibling HMAC authenticator** `jwtSession.go`, which has an empty-key forgery bug.
- It added `validatePathComponent` for the `machine_state` REST handlers — but missed the **metric-ingestion path** that builds filesystem paths from the same kind of input.

---

### 🔴 CRITICAL 1 — Unauthenticated admin takeover via empty-key HMAC JWT (`internal/auth/jwtSession.go`)

`JWTSessionAuthenticator.Init()` (line 27) returns `nil` even when `CROSS_LOGIN_JWT_HS512_KEY` is unset, so the authenticator is **registered with `loginTokenKey == nil`** whenever the `jwts` config block exists (confirmed at `auth.go:233-238`). In `Login()` (line 61-66) the keyfunc returns that nil key for HS256/HS512 tokens. golang-jwt v5.3.1 happily verifies an HMAC signature against an empty key.

I confirmed with a PoC against the pinned v5.3.1:
```
forged token: eyJhbGciOiJIUzUxMiIs...
RESULT: FORGED TOKEN ACCEPTED sub=attacker roles=[admin]
```
`CanLogin` accepts any request with an `Authorization` header or `?login-token=`, and with the default `validate-user: false`, `getUserFromJWT` builds the user — **including roles — straight from the attacker's claims** (`jwtHelpers.go:125-137`). 

**Exploit:** unauthenticated attacker forges `HS512({"sub":"x","roles":["admin"]})` signed with an empty key, sends it as `Authorization: Bearer …`, and receives a full admin session. With `validate-user: true` it still allows impersonation of any existing user. Precondition (JWT configured but no HS512 cross-login key) is a common deployment.

**Fix:** `Init()` must `return error` when the key is empty (so it isn't registered), and the keyfunc should reject `len(loginTokenKey)==0` — mirroring how `jwtCookieSession.go` already guards its prerequisites.

---

### 🔴 CRITICAL 2 — SQL injection via GraphQL metric name (`internal/repository/stats.go:959-969`, `jobQuery.go:343`)

Metric names from the GraphQL `[String!]` args (`jobsStatistics`/`jobsMetricStats` `metrics`, and `JobFilter.metricStats.metricName`) are interpolated **raw** into a `json_extract(footprint, "$.<name>")` SQL string. No whitelist exists anywhere in the chain (`metrics` → `AddMetricHistograms` → `jobsMetricStatisticsHistogram(ctx, m, …)`, verified). SQLite treats `"$.x"` as a string literal, so a `"` breaks out.

Confirmed exfiltration PoC in SQLite:
```
metric = cpu_avg") UNION SELECT secret,1 FROM job--
→ returns TOP-SECRET-DATA
```
Reachable by **any authenticated RoleUser**; `SecurityCheck` only adds a row predicate that `UNION`/`--` discards. Result: arbitrary SQL read across all tables (other users' jobs, `hpc_user` roles/emails).

**Fix:** whitelist metric names against the cluster's configured metric list, plus a strict `^[A-Za-z0-9_]+$` guard at the interpolation sites.

---

### 🟠 HIGH 3 — Arbitrary file write outside checkpoint root via metric ingestion (`pkg/metricstore/lineprotocol.go` → `walCheckpoint.go:246`, `checkpoint.go:251/347`)

`DecodeLine` reads `cluster`/`hostname` line-protocol tags with **no validation** (only the measurement name is whitelisted). They flow verbatim into `path.Join(RootDir, msg.Cluster, msg.Node)` and then `os.MkdirAll` + `os.OpenFile(O_CREATE|O_TRUNC)`. `path.Join` cleans but does **not** confine, so `cluster=../../../../var/spool/cron` escapes the root.

Reachable **unauthenticated via NATS** (`ReceiveNats`) and via `POST /api/write` (RoleAPI). On the next checkpoint flush the server creates/truncates files outside the archive root under the service UID → potential code execution depending on deployment. The fix already exists in-repo (`validatePathComponent`, added by the prior audit for `machine_state`) — it just wasn't applied at ingestion.

**Fix:** validate `cluster`/`host` in `DecodeLine` (reject `..`, `/`, `\`, restrict charset), and add a defense-in-depth root-prefix check at the sinks.

---

### Medium (worth fixing, not release-blocking)

- **No GraphQL complexity/depth limit + `items-per-page=-1` bypasses LIMIT** (`server.go:89`, `jobQuery.go:78`, REST `job.go:170`) — any authenticated user can force an unbounded DB + metric-store load (DoS). Negative values other than `-1` underflow `uint64`.
- **Missing security headers** (`server.go`) — no CSP, `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `Referrer-Policy` (only HSTS). Enables clickjacking and amplifies any XSS.
- **Stored XSS sink** `{@html job.metaData.message}` (`web/frontend/src/Job.root.svelte:240`, `JobFootprint.svelte:231`) — writable via `/api/jobs/edit_meta` (RoleAPI) or a hand-edited archive `meta.json`; executes in any viewer's browser. Combined with no CSP, unconstrained.
- **Identity values interpolated into SQL** in tag counting (`tags.go:316-333`) — `user.Username`/`user.Projects` concatenated into SQL; low-risk for local accounts but exploitable for OIDC/LDAP-provisioned names containing `"`.
- **CSRF**: state-changing session-auth endpoints rely solely on `SameSite=Lax` (no token / Origin check).

### Low
ORDER BY uses a fragile char *blacklist* not a column allow-list (`jobQuery.go` `toSnakeCase`); default JWT keypair only warns instead of being fatal (`main.go:217`); committed demo secrets/JWT; `AuthViaJWT` skips role validation (`jwt.go:114`); no local-password strength policy.

---

The two CRITICALs (and the HIGH) are the genuinely severe items the prior audit missed, and all three are the "same bug class, different file" pattern noted above — so the fixes are mostly mechanical extensions of what's already there.

Want me to implement the fixes? I'd suggest starting with the three top items (JWT Init guard, metric-name whitelist, ingestion path validation). I can do them as one branch with tests, or one commit per finding — your call.

All metric names use only `[a-z0-9_]` — my regex won't reject any legitimate names.

## Done — all three top items fixed

Build passes, `go vet` is clean, and the auth/repository/metricstore test suites all pass.

### 🔴 1. JWT empty-key auth bypass — `internal/auth/jwtSession.go`
- `Init()` now returns an error when `CROSS_LOGIN_JWT_HS512_KEY` is unset, so the authenticator is **no longer registered** without a key (instead of registering with a nil key). `auth.go` already handles that error by logging "No JWT login support!" and skipping registration.
- Added defense-in-depth in the keyfunc: rejects HS256/HS512 verification when `len(loginTokenKey) == 0`.

Re-ran my forgery PoC mentally against the new path: a key-less deployment no longer registers the authenticator, and even if reached, the empty-key guard rejects the token.

### 🔴 2. SQL injection via metric name — `internal/repository/stats.go`, `jobQuery.go`
- Added `validMetricName = ^[a-zA-Z0-9_]+$`.
- `jobsMetricStatisticsHistogram` returns an error for invalid metric names before any SQL interpolation.
- `buildFloatJSONCondition` (the `metricStats` filter path) fails closed with `WHERE 0 = 1` on an invalid name.
- Verified these are the only two `json_extract(footprint, "$.<name>")` interpolation sites; the running-jobs histogram builds bins in Go, not SQL. Confirmed all real metric names in the repo match the whitelist.

### 🟠 3. Path traversal in metric ingestion — `pkg/metricstore/lineprotocol.go`
- Added `isValidPathComponent()` (rejects `..`, `/`, `\`) and applied it to `cluster`/`host` right after tag parsing in `DecodeLine`, before they become tree keys or checkpoint/WAL path components. Offending samples are skipped with a warning; the rest of the batch proceeds. This blocks both the unauthenticated NATS path and `POST /api/write`.

I followed the existing in-repo conventions (mirrors `validatePathComponent` in `rest.go`, fail-closed query building, `cclog.Warnf` logging).

content.text:
All medium-severity items are fixed. Backend builds, `go vet` is clean, all tests pass, and the frontend bundle rebuilds successfully.

## Medium-severity fixes

**1. GraphQL DoS + unbounded pagination** — `cmd/cc-backend/server.go`, `internal/repository/jobQuery.go`, `internal/api/job.go`
- Added `extension.FixedComplexityLimit(5000)` to bound nested/aliased query cost.
- Hardened `QueryJobs`: rejects non-positive `items-per-page` (except the `-1` "load all" sentinel) so `uint64()` can't underflow into a giant limit; clamps `page` to ≥1 to avoid offset underflow.
- REST `getJobs` now returns `400` for `page`/`items-per-page` < 1 instead of passing them downstream. (GraphQL `-1` "Load All" is preserved — it's an intentional dashboard feature; the underflow guard still protects against other negatives.)

**2. Missing security headers** — `cmd/cc-backend/server.go`
- Added `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, `X-Frame-Options: DENY`, `Referrer-Policy: same-origin`, and a conservative CSP (`frame-ancestors 'none'; object-src 'none'; base-uri 'self'`). I deliberately kept the CSP non-script-restrictive so it can't break the self-hosted SPA's inline scripts — a full `script-src` policy needs per-template nonces and is best done as a separate, tested change.

**3. Stored XSS via admin note** — `web/frontend/src/Job.root.svelte`, `JobFootprint.svelte`
- Replaced both `{@html job.metaData.message}` sinks with escaped text in a `white-space: pre-wrap` span — kills script execution while preserving line-break readability. No new dependency (no DOMPurify in the project).

**4. SQL identity-value interpolation** — `internal/repository/tags.go`
- Parameterized the tag-scope `IN` list (was concatenating `user.Username`) via `sq.Eq`, and the manager project subquery (was `strings.Join`) via a `?`-placeholder list. Also fixed a latent bug: a manager with no projects now correctly counts own jobs instead of generating `IN ("")`.

**5. CSRF defense-in-depth** — `cmd/cc-backend/server.go`
- Added a `Sec-Fetch-Site: cross-site` block on state-changing methods. It fails open when the header is absent, so non-browser API clients (schedulers, cc-metric-store, curl) and the same-origin SPA are unaffected; it only blocks attacker-origin browser requests, complementing the existing SameSite=Lax cookie.

I haven't committed. Want these as one commit (same style as before), or split (e.g. backend hardening vs. frontend XSS)?
